analogy: Think of "Distribution of Oceans and Continents" through the chapter's own opening images: In the previous chapter, you have studied the interior of the earth. You know that continents cover 29 per cent of the surface of the earth and the remainder is under oceanic waters. The positions of the continents and the ocean bodies, as we see... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Distribution of Oceans and Continents is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that in the previous chapter, you have studied the interior of the earth.

The text explains that you know that continents cover 29 per cent of the surface of the earth and the remainder is under oceanic waters.

The text explains that the positions of the continents and the ocean bodies, as we see them in the map, have not been the same in the past.

The text explains that moreover, it is now a well-accepted fact that oceans and continents will not continue to enjoy their present positions in times to come.

The text explains that if this is so, the question arises what were their positions in the past?

The text explains that even if it is true that the continents and oceans have changed and are changing their positions, you may wonder as to how scientists know this.

The text explains that how have they determined their earlier positions?

The text explains that you will find the answers to some of these and related questions in this chapter.

The text explains that cONTINENTAL DRIFT Observe the shape of the coastline of the Atlantic Ocean.

The text explains that you will be surprised by the symmetry of the coastlines on either side of the ocean.

The text explains that no wonder, many scientists thought of this similarity and considered the possibility of the two Americas, Europe and Africa, to be once joined together.

The text explains that from the known records of the history of science, it was Abraham Ortelius, a Dutch map maker, who first proposed such a possibility as early as 1596.

The text explains that antonio Pellegrini drew a map showing the three continents together.

The text explains that however, it was Alfred Wegener—a German meteorologist who put forth a comprehensive argument in the form of “the continental drift DISTRIBUTION OF OCEANS AND CONTINENTS C H A P T E R theory” in 1912.

The text explains that this was regarding the distribution of the oceans and the continents.

The text explains that according to Wegener, all the continents formed a single continental mass and mega ocean surrounded the same.

The text explains that rocks of Same Age Across the Oceans The radiometric dating methods developed in the recent period have facilitated correlating the rock formation from different continents across

The text explains that the belt of ancient rocks of 2,000 million years from Brazil coast matches with those from western Africa.

The text explains that tillite It is the sedimentary rock formed out of deposits of glaciers.

The text explains that placer Deposits The occurrence of rich placer deposits of gold in the Ghana coast and the absolute absence of source rock in the region is an amazing fact.

The text explains that convectional Current Theory Arthur Holmes in 1930s discussed the possibility of convection currents operating in the mantle portion.

The text explains that these currents are generated due to radioactive elements causing thermal differences in the mantle portion.

The text explains that holmes argued that there exists a system of such currents in the entire mantle portion.

The text explains that the mid-oceanic ridges were found to be most active in terms of volcanic eruptions.

The text explains that the dating of the rocks from the oceanic crust revealed the fact that they are much younger than the continental areas.

The text explains that rocks on either side of the crest of oceanic ridges and having equi-distant locations from the crest were found to have remarkable similarities both in terms of their constituents and their age.

The text explains that the rift system at the crest is the zone of intense volcanic activity.

The text explains that in the previous chapter, you have been introduced to this type of volcanoes as mid- oceanic volcanoes.

The text explains that distribution of Earthquakes and V Distribution of Earthquakes and V Distribution of Earthquakes and V Distribution of Earthquakes and V Distribution of Earthquakes and Volcanoes olcanoes olcanoes olcanoes olcanoes Study the maps showing the distribution of seismic activity and volcanoes given in Figure 4.2.

The text explains that in general, the foci of the earthquake in the areas of mid-oceanic ridges are at shallow depths whereas along the Alpine-Himalayan belt as well as the rim of the Pacific, the earthquakes are deep-seated ones.

The text explains that the map of volcanoes also shows a similar pattern.

The text explains that the rim of the Pacific is also called rim of fire due to the existence of active volcanoes in this area.

The text explains that particularly, the mapping of the ocean floor and palaeomagnetic studies of rocks from oceanic regions revealed the following facts : (i) It was realised that all along the mid- oceanic ridges, volcanic eruptions are common and they bring huge amounts of lava to the surface in this area.

The text explains that (ii) The rocks equidistant on either sides of the crest of mid-oceanic ridges show remarkable similarities in terms of period of formation, chemical compositions and magnetic properties.

The text explains that rocks closer to the mid-oceanic ridges have normal polarity and are the youngest.

The text explains that the age of the rocks increases as one moves away from the crest.

The text explains that (iii) The ocean crust rocks are much younger than the continental rocks.

The text explains that the age of rocks in the oceanic crust is nowhere more than 200 million years old.

The text explains that some of the continental rock formations are as old as 3,200 million years.

The text explains that (v) The deep trenches have deep-seated earthquake occurrences while in the mid- oceanic ridge areas, the quake foci have shallow depths.

The text explains that these facts and a detailed analysis of magnetic properties of the rocks on either sides of the mid-oceanic ridge led Hess (1961) to propose his hypothesis, known as the “sea floor spreading”.

The text explains that hess argued that constant eruptions at the crest of oceanic ridges cause the rupture of the oceanic crust and the new lava wedges into it, pushing the oceanic crust on either side.

The text explains that the younger age of the oceanic crust as well as the fact that the spreading of one ocean does not cause the shrinking of the other, made Hess Figure 4.

The text explains that 2 : Distribution of earthquakes and volcanoes

The text explains that dISTRIBUTION OF OCEANS AND CONTINENTS 31 think about the consumption of the oceanic crust.

The text explains that he further maintained that the ocean floor that gets pushed due to volcanic eruptions at the crest, sinks down at the oceanic trenches and gets consumed.

The text explains that a tectonic plate (also called lithospheric plate) is a massive, irregularly-shaped slab of solid rock, generally composed of both continental and oceanic lithosphere.

The text explains that the lithosphere includes the crust and top mantle with its thickness range varying between 5 and100 km in oceanic parts and about 200 km in the continental areas.

The text explains that position of the Indian sub- continent (mostly Peninsular India) is traced with the help of the rocks analysed from the Nagpur area.

The text explains that there are three types of plate boundaries: Divergent Boundaries Where new crust is generated as the plates pull away from each other.

The text explains that convergent Boundaries Where the crust is destroyed as one plate dived under another.

The text explains that (c) Distribution of fossils in different continents.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 4: Distribution of Oceans and Continents

prelims_traps: Do not treat CONTINENTAL DRIFT and DISTRIBUTION OF OCEANS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Distribution of Oceans and Continents; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Distribution of Oceans and Continents through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CONTINENTAL DRIFT and DISTRIBUTION OF OCEANS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

Subsequently, Laurasia and Gondwanaland continued to break into various smaller continents that exist today.
